howItWorks.intro: This uv exposure calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Burn time = Skin type base (minutes) ÷ UV Index
Protected time = Burn time × SPF × 0.5 (real-world factor)
Capped at 8 hours — reapply sunscreen every 2 hours regardless
UV Index 1-2: Low, 3-5: Moderate, 6-7: High, 8-10: Very High, 11+: Extreme
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: What UV index is dangerous?
faq.answer: UV 1-2: Low risk. UV 3-5: Moderate, wear sunscreen. UV 6-7: High, reduce midday exposure. UV 8-10: Very High, avoid 10am-4pm sun. UV 11+: Extreme, stay in shade. Most US summer days reach UV 7-10.

faq.question: Does SPF 100 give twice the protection of SPF 50?
faq.answer: No. SPF 30 blocks 97% of UVB, SPF 50 blocks 98%, SPF 100 blocks 99%. The difference is minimal. More important: apply enough (1 oz for full body), reapply every 2 hours, and reapply after swimming/sweating.

faq.question: Can you get sunburned on cloudy days?
faq.answer: Yes. Up to 80% of UV rays penetrate clouds. You can also burn in the shade due to reflected UV from sand (25%), water (10%), concrete (12%), and snow (80%). UV exposure is cumulative throughout the day.
